6166566.title: Introduction
6166566.transcript: Hi, my name is Balazs from Budapest, Hungary and welcome to my course on multithreading and parallel computing in Java. First of all, let me tell a few words about myself. I'm from Budapest, Hungary. I am qualified as a physicist and my main interests are algorithms, data structures, and how to design maintainable and scalable softwares. So what you are going to get from this course, we are going to talk about several topics on a step by step basis. So first we consider what are threads. How to create threads with multiple approaches. What is thread synchronization and why it is crucial. Then we will talk about synchronized collections and synchronized data structures. It is another crucial topic related to multi-threading. Then we will talk about parallelization and how to design parallel algorithms. We will implement the parallel merge sort algorithm on a step by step basis. Then we will talk about the fork join framework. And we will come to the conclusion that it is quite easy and convenient to create parallel algorithms with the help of this framework. And finally, we will talk about the stream API and how to process collections of objects efficiently. Of course, we will talk about several other topics such as the dining philosophers problem or for example, virtual threads. Because Java virtual threads change the concurrency model by enabling lightweight, scalable threads managed by the Java Virtual Machine. And we will come to the conclusion that it is extremely powerful. So these are the topics we are going to talk about in a step by step basis. Thanks for joining my course. Let's get started.

6166580.title: Processes and threads introduction
6166580.transcript: In this lecture. We are going to talk about that. What is the motivation behind threads? So why do we need to deal with Multithreading at all? So first of all, programming languages are sequential by default. What does it mean? It means that they execute the commands and operations one by one on a line by line basis. So what does it mean exactly? If we have the first operation, then it is going to be executed. Then the next operation will be executed, then the third operation will be executed and so on. So these operations are going to be executed one by one. So for example, if we have the main method in Java and we have four method calls, first of all, we initializing the data structure, then we download data from the web, then we build a given modal, and then finally we make predictions. Then what's going to happen? Java is going to consider the main method and it is going to execute the methods one by one. First of all, it is going to initialize the arrays. After that, it is going to download data from the web. After that it is going to build the model and finally it is going to make predictions. So this is a sequential execution. So in a single threaded application, these operations are executed one after the other. You may guess that there is going to be some problem. So the problem is that time consuming operations may freeze the entire application. For example, when we try to download a huge data set from the web, if you are dealing with, for example, machine learning algorithms, then of course you have to wait for 10 to 20 minutes in order to download the given data that you are going to handle, which means that it is an extremely time consuming operation and it may freeze the entire application. So if we have just a single threaded application, then because Java executes the operations in a sequential manner, then essentially all the other operations have to wait for the download operation to be finished. And of course it means that the application freezes, The user doesn't know what's happening exactly. He or she has to wait for minutes to continue with the execution of the other operations. So this is why a single threaded application is not that practical. Okay, so the most crucial reason for multithreading and to use multiple threads in a given application is to separate multiple time consuming tasks. So tasks that might be subject to interference by the execution of other tasks. So for example, we have the application that runs on the user's computer and we want to download a huge data set. So let's assume that we are dealing with a stock market application and our application downloads the real time data from example from Yahoo Finance, and then it constructs time series models based on the dataset that's going to be downloaded. Of course, the problem that it takes 2-3 minutes to fetch the huge dataset from Yahoo Finance. So again, the application on the user's computer is going to freeze because the application on the user's computer has to wait for the data to be downloaded. And of course the solution is to use multithreading. So essentially we have to use a distinct thread for this time consuming operation. A good example is copying directories. For example, I'm going to copy a huge directory and as you can see during this procedure, the operating system doesn't freeze as you can see. So this is exactly why multithreading is extremely powerful, because here this operation as far as copying all the items are concerned, are handled by a different thread, which means that it is totally independent from the main thread. Of course it is just for demonstration purposes, so I'm not going to finish the operation. Okay. So multithreading is the ability of the central processing unit to execute multiple processes or threads simultaneously. You may pose the question that what are processes and what are threads? Both threads and processes are independent sequences of execution. Let's talk about them one by one. So first of all, there are processes. What is a process? A process is an instance of a program execution. So essentially this is a fundamental unit of work in our operating system. For example, when you open a given software such as Paint, Excel or Adobe Photoshop, for example, or a given web browser, these are distinct applications and the operating system is. Going to assign a distinct process to every single application. By the way, the operating system assigns distinct registers, stack memory and heap memory to every single process. So what does it mean exactly that we have the given process and within the given process, there are the registers, there is the so called stack memory, there is the so called heap memory and so on. So these resources are associated with the given process. By the way, in Java we can create processes with the help of the ProcessBuilder class. By the way, in the common line, if you run the command task list, then it's going to show all the operating system related processes. As you can see, one process for the common line itself. There are several processes for every single Chrome tab. I use several pages in Chrome, so that's why there are several Chrome.exe related processes, as you can see here as well. There's going to be a distinct process for Eclipse, I mean the integrated development environment for Java. So every single application you run, there's going to be a distinct process in the operating system. Every single process has a PID, so the process identification number, we can track the memory usage. As you can see, every single process uses some memory. So every single application and software is a distinct process. What's crucial that usually we are not dealing with processes because creating a new process is quite expensive in the sense that we have to allocate registers, we have to allocate stack memory and heap memory. And what's crucial that the processes within a given operating system form a directed graph in the sense that every single process has a so called parent process. So whenever we create a new process, then essentially we have to handle the parent process as well. So it is quite expensive to create a new process. But luckily there are the so called threads. A given thread is essentially a lightweight process. A thread is again a unit of execution, but what's crucial that threads exist within given processes. So a single process may contain one or more threads, and every single thread in a given process shares the memory and resources of that given process. And this is why programmers have to deal with concurrent programming. So what does it mean exactly that we have the given process with the given resources? This is what we have been talking about a few minutes earlier, that every single process has its own registers, stack memory and heap memory, and every single process may contain one or more threads. So we have the first thread, we have the second thread, we have the third thread, and we can create as many threads associated with a given process as we want. So why is it so powerful? Because creating new threads require fewer resources than creating new processes. You may guess that creating a new thread is quite easy because we don't have to deal with the parent of that given process. We don't have to allocate new registers. So this is why threads are so called lightweight processes. Creating a new thread is way more easier and require fewer resources than creating new processes. On the other hand, each thread in the process shares the stack memory, the heap memory and all the other resources. So this is why dealing with multiple threads is not that easy. Okay, so in this lecture we came to the conclusion that why is it so crucial to use multiple threads? Because we want to avoid our application to freeze. So this is why it is advisable to use multiple threads for time consuming operations. And then we came to the conclusion that because multiple threads exist within a given process and each thread in the process shares the memory, this is why we have to deal with concurrent programming, because of course the content of the memory, for example, may become inconsistent. The first thread can manipulate the memory, the second thread can manipulate the same memory location, the third thread can manipulate the same memory location and so on. So this is why we have to make sure that the data we are storing within a given process is going to be consistent. So this is why we have to deal with synchronization. This is why we have to deal with concurrent programming and so on. In the next lecture, we are going to talk about the time-slicing algorithm. Thanks for watching.

6175928.title: Deadlock and livelock introduction
6175928.transcript: In this lecture we are going to talk about deadlocks and live locks. So first of all, what is a deadlock? A deadlock in Java occurs when two or more threads are waiting for each other to release resources, such as the intrinsic clock, and none of them ever does. As a result, all of the threads involved in the deadlock are stuck forever. So what does it mean exactly that we have a given thread? This is the first thread. We have another thread, the second thread, and for example, the first thread is waiting for the second thread to release the lock, while the second thread is waiting for the first thread to release the lock. This is a deadlock situation that they are waiting for each other to release resources. And of course, in this case the resource is the intrinsic lock itself. And of course, in this case these threads are stuck forever. So neither of them can proceed because they are each waiting for a resource. The other thread holds. What can be the solution? We can use the lock interface and we have been talking about the reentrant lock in the previous lectures, and we came to the conclusion that we can use the Trylock method. That is quite powerful. Why? Because instead of trying to acquire the intrinsic lock indefinitely, we can try to acquire that given lock. And if it is not possible to do so, then the thread is not going to wait for that lock to be released. So this is how we can avoid threads being stuck forever. By the way, it is crucial that in this case the threads are blocked so they are not executing any operations. On the other hand, what about live locks? Live lock is a situation where two or more threads keep changing their state in response to each other, but never make actual progress similar to deadlock, but the threads are not blocked, just too busy reacting to each other to finish. By the way, it is very similar to the following situation. Let's assume that two people, Alice and Bob, are walking toward each other in a narrow hallway that only allow one person to pass at a time. They both try to avoid blocking each other, so Alice steps to the left. Bob steps to the right, but that ends up being the same side, so they both realize they are still blocking each other. They both step to the opposite side again, the same side. So they keep switching sides, trying to let the other pass but never actually move forward. And this is what I am trying to illustrate on the left that the threads are busy. In this case they are not blocked. They are busy reacting to each other to finish execution And they want to acquire the lock that the other thread holds. Okay. So this is the Livelock situation. No matter, we are dealing with deadlock or livelock, the threads cannot proceed to execute the specific tasks. By the way, the solution is to use re-entrant locks again with the triple lock method. So let's take a look at the comparison. In the deadlock situation, the threads are blocked and they are doing nothing. It is quite the opposite when dealing with livelock. The threads are running but not making progress. With deadlock these threads are waiting for the lock. With livelock each thread keeps responding to others. So with deadlock the threads are frozen. On the other hand, with Livelock the threads are active but stuck in a loop. No matter, we are dealing with deadlock or livelock Lock situation. The solution is approximately the same. First of all, we have to use the lock interface and usually the re-entrant lock implementation. So this is how we can ensure that the thread is not blocked indefinitely if it cannot acquire the lock. This is exactly why we can use the Trylock method that tries to acquire the lock, but if it is not possible to do so, it gets back and doesn't wait indefinitely. So using the re-entrant lock implementation is definitely a good idea. Then we have to avoid cyclic dependencies. We should ensure that each thread acquires the locks in the same order to avoid any cyclic dependency in lock acquisition. By the way, this is a typical cyclic dependency that Thread2 tries to acquire. The first lock that the first thread is holding, and the first thread is trying to get the lock that the second thread is holding. So essentially they are waiting for each other to release the lock. This is a typical case of a cyclic dependency. And of course we have to avoid that. And finally we have to introduce some randomness. So livelock can be handled using the same approaches. I mean that we should avoid cyclic dependencies. And we should use the re-entrant lock implementation. And the threads should retry acquiring the locks at random intervals. So instead of trying to acquire the lock indefinitely, the first thread and the second thread can try to acquire the lock for a short period of time. And if it is not possible to do so, then they get back and they do not wait for that given lock indefinitely, and then after some random time, they can try again. 6176010.title: What is parallel computing?
6176010.transcript: In the coming lectures, we are going to talk about parallel computation. So in this lecture, we are going to talk about what is parallel computing when we use standard sequential approach than the programming language executes the operations one after another. So if we have, for example, a single threaded sequential machine learning related approach that downloads the data from the web then removes the invalid values from the data set, then finds a linear regression model just for demonstration purposes. And finally, it makes some prediction on a test dataset. It is a typical sequential approach. Of course, the given steps are not independent of each other. So, for example, we are not able to fit the linear regression model. If we do not have a dataset or, for example, we are not able to preprocess the dataset and remove the invalid values before downloading the data from the web. So, of course, most of the problems can be solved with the help of sequential approaches. Multi-threaded approach uses multiple threads, but these threads are executed with that time-slicing algorithm, so multithreading is used to execute independent tasks without blocking other operations. So this is exactly what we have been talking about. That, for example, if we have an application that keeps downloading data from the web, we would like to make sure that during this download operation, the application itself is not going to freeze. Or, for example, if we want to copy a huge file in our file system, then of course, we would like to make sure that during this operation, we can do other tasks. And of course, the operating system is not going to freeze because it uses a distinct thread for this given operation. So multi-threading is used to execute independent tasks without blocking other tasks. With the help of parallel algorithms and parallelisation, we can execute different tasks on different processors or processor cores simultaneously. So parallelization is used to speed up a given application. This is a typical interview question whether we can speed up a given application with multi-threading. And of course, the answer is no. The aim of multithreading is not to speed up the application itself. If we want to boost our application so we want to reduce the running time, then we have to use parallelization which means that we run the tasks on different processors or processor cores simultaneously, so multiple tasks are going to be executed at the same time. By the way, some problems are sequential by default, so we are not able to apply parallelisation OK, so if we take a look at this implementation that it is clear that what is the difference between parallel execution and multithreaded execution? This is exactly what we have been talking about in the first chapter that this is the time-slicing algorithm. If we have just a single processor and let's assume that we are using two threads, then of course, both of the operations cannot be executed at the same time because we have just a single processor. So this is why the single processor will execute the first thread for a short amount of time. then the second thread, then the first thread again, the second thread, the first thread, the second thread. This is the time-slicing algorithm. Both of the threads are going to be executed, but not at the same time. As far as parallelization is concerned, because we are dealing with multiple processors, every single thread is executed by a different processor or processor core. So the first thread is executed by the first processor and the second thread is executed by the second processor. So as you can see, these operations can be executed simultaneously at the same time. So this is the crucial difference between parallel execution and the multi-threaded execution. And of course, this is why parallel execution can speed up the running time of a given application. OK, so the motivation for parallel algorithms is that we have multiple processor cores in modern machines and computers. We can divide the problem into multiple independent subsets. This is what we are going to do when dealing with merge sort, and we can execute the operations on these subsets simultaneously. So at the same time, such as, for example, we can check prime numbers within a given interview with the help of parallelization We can do sorting such as a merged sort or quick sort with the help of parallelization. We can implement search algorithms in a parallel manner and so on. So this is exactly what we are going to do. We will divide the problem into multiple independent subsets and we are. going to assign these subsets to the independent processors, and these processors are going to execute these threads independently at the same time. On the other hand, with sequential approaches. The steps are usually depending on each other. We are not able to execute the operations independently. This is exactly what we have been talking about a few minutes earlier that if we want to download data from the web, if we want to transform these data and then we want to fit some a model such as linear regression. Then of course, these tasks cannot be executed simultaneously because if we want to pre-processed the data first, we need the data itself. If we want to fit a linear regression model, then of course we need the preprocessed dataset. So we have no other option about to use sequential approach, which means that first we download the data data, we pre-processed the data and finally, we fit the linear regression model. A typical example is numerical methods, such as solving differential equations, such as with Euler's method or Runge-Kutta method. These are typical sequential algorithms. We can calculate the next step based on the previous step of the algorithm, which means that these steps are depending on each other. We are not able to use parallelization. And by the way, it is absolutely crucial that it depends on the underlying operating system how these threads will be executed because usually on modern machines, we have several processors or processor cores. For example, all of my machine there are eight processor cores, which means that eight threads can be executed simultaneously at the same time. So if there is just a single processor core, then the time slicing algorithm executes the threads in a multi-threaded application. But if there are multiple processor cores, then the operating system may execute the threads in parallel. Despite the fact that we have implemented just the multi-strategy application. So if we have multiple threads, then the operating system may optimize our application in the sense that because it knows that it has several available processors, it will run the threads on different processor cores, which means that we may end up with a parallel execution despite the fact that we had no intention to do so. So this is why several students posed the question that why are the tasks are executed in a parallel manner despite the fact that we are using multithreading? And the answer is that the operating system may decide whether to run the threads independently on the multiple processors or using the time-slicing algorithm. OK, and finally, let's talk about that. What is the problem with parallel computing? There are two crucial problems. First of all, communication between the threads. For sequential algorithms, we measure the running time and memory complexity of the algorithms. I'm not sure whether you are familiar with data structures or algorithms, such as graph algorithms depth-first search, Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm and so on. Usually, we can describe these kinds of sequential approaches with the help of so-called running time complexities. For example, if we are looking for an unknown item in a one dimensional array, then the running time complexities is O(N) linear. If we are looking for an item in a sorted one dimensional array, then we can do you or do O(logN) logarithmic running time complexity with a binary search. But as far as parallel execution is concerned, we have to take the communication factor between the threads into consideration. This is the so-called parallel slowdown, so it is quite expensive to communicate between threads that are being executed. And this is why we will come to the conclusion that if we have a small problem than the parallel execution, we will be slower than the sequential execution. Of course, if we have an extremely large problem, for example, we would like to sort ten billion items. This is when parallelization can prove to be extremely powerful. So this is the so-called parallel slowdown. OK, so this is the first problem when dealing with parallelization that we have to consider communication factors. Then we have to consider the load balance problem. So we have to make sure to split the work evenly among the processors. You may guess that it is not always straightforward. For example, if we want to find the prime factors and this is quite crucial when dealing with cryptography over the first N integers, for example, we would like to find the prime factors of integers is 0, 1, 2 ... up to 1000. But of course, we can come to the conclusion that finding the prime factors is harder if the given integer is larger, which means that the first thread will execute the operation faster. And this is the typical load balance problem. So, of course, the second strand will do more work, while on the other hand, the first thread will do nothing after finishing the computation. OK, so this is a crucial problem of parallelization that how to make sure to split the work evenly. Among the process source, we should make sure that the processors shouldn't wait for each other to finish every process or should do the same amount of work. 6177194.title: Downside of multithreading
6177194.transcript: In this lecture, we are going to talk about the disadvantages of multithreading. So first of all, creating and using multiple threads is not always the best solution possible. So multithreading has several disadvantages as well. The first problem is that threads are manipulating data that are located on the same memory area because they belong to the same process. So this is what we have been talking about in the previous lectures, that there may be multiple threads associated with a given process. The process itself has registers, stack memory, heap memory and so on, and all the threads are sharing these resources. And of course because they share the same memory area, this is why we have to deal with synchronization. This is what we are going to talk about in the coming lectures that we have to make sure that multiple threads are not going to manipulate the same data. Why? Because the data may become inconsistent if multiple threads are manipulating at the same time. And of course, synchronization is not that straightforward. So usually software engineers do not like synchronization and multithreading because it is not that easy. So this is the first disadvantage of multithreading. Another disadvantage is that it is not that easy to design and test multithreaded applications. So for example, if there is a bug in the multithreaded application, it is extremely hard to find out what is the problem exactly. By the way, we are going to talk about the fork-join framework and it is going to be a bit easier to design and test multithreaded applications. But anyways, multithreaded applications are hard to design and test and finally using multiple threads is expensive. So the central processing unit has to save local data such as the application pointer or for example, the memory location of the actual thread and has to load the other thread. So for example, when there are three threads that are executed by a single central processing unit, then every time there is a switch between the threads, the central processing unit has to save the actual thread and has to load the context of the next thread. Then again, then again, again, again. So this is why switching between threads is extremely expensive. Okay, so it is expensive to switch between multiple threads. This is why an algorithm may become too slow with multiple threads. For example, we will come to the conclusion that if we want to implement merge sort, then using multiple threads is a good idea. But there are other algorithms where using multiple threads is going to make the algorithm slower. So it is not always true that using multiple threads will decrease the running time. So you can take a look at the illustration and we can come to the conclusion that using multiple threads can be faster than just a single threaded application. So as you can see, just a few number of threads can decrease the running time, but if we use a huge number of threads, then the running time will increase dramatically. We can come to the conclusion that with the help of a huge number of threads, the running time will be slower than the standard single threaded application. So it is not a good idea to use a huge number of threads. A rule of thumb is that for small problems and small applications, it is unnecessary to use multiple threads. By the way, it is true for most of the algorithms, algorithms and data structures are designed to be sequential. There are some algorithms that can be run in parallel with multiple threads, such as, for example, merge sort or quicksort, but most of the algorithms are inherently sequential. Okay. So the rule of thumb is that with the help of just a few threads, usually we can decrease the running time, but using too many threads will increase the running time dramatically. Okay, that's all about the disadvantages. Thanks for watching.

So first of all, we can achieve everything with the help of re-entrant clocks that we have seen with the synchronized approach. But there are several additional features such as for example, we can use Trylock method that lets you attempt to get the lock without blocking. Of course, it is not possible with synchronized. Then we can define a timeout that allows waiting for a certain time. Of course it is not possible with synchronized approach. Then we can use this lock interrupt method that lets a thread be interrupted while waiting for the lock itself. On the other hand, a thread is blocked until lock is acquired and cannot be interrupted with the synchronized approach. Then what about the fairness? There's no fairness guarantee when dealing with the synchronized approach. On the other hand, we can weekend. Create a fair look. When we instantiate a new re-entrant look, we have to define true as the Boolean variable to grant access in request order. Then we can use multiple condition objects with re-entrant locks. On the other hand, there is only one wait set per object using the wait and notify methods with the synchronized approach. And finally, you can manually lock and unlock when dealing with reentrant locks. On the other hand, the lock is automatically managed by the Java Virtual machine when dealing with the synchronized keyword. So these are the crucial differences. And we can come to the conclusion that we should use re-entrant locks because we can achieve everything that is possible with the synchronized keyword. But there are several additional methods. And we will come to the conclusion that it is easier to avoid deadlocks and live locks because of the methods that are available in the re-entrant lock class.

6270786.title: What is the Fork-join framework?
6270786.transcript: In this chapter and in the coming lectures, we are going to talk about the Fork-Join framework. OK, so first of all, why do we have to talk about the Fork-Join framework? Fork-Join framework is an implementation of the ExecutorService interface for parallel execution. So we have been talking about parallelization and we came to the conclusion that it is not that straightforward. If we want to implement it from scratch, we have to understand thread scheduling. We have to make sure that the threads are going to wait for each other to finish with the execution and so on. Multi-threading in the main is quite hard because it is extremely hard to debug multi-threaded code. It is extremely hard to make sure that they are not going to be any bugs and so on. This is the same for parallel execution, and this is why the Fork-Join framework is so convenient because we don't have to implement parallelization from scratch. The Fork-Join framework is going to do everything for us. OK, so this is the implementation of the ExecutorService This is exactly what we have discussed in the previous chapters. OK, so the Fork-Join framework has to make an agreed the parallel. And of course, we don't have to deal with low level synchronization and locks. It is a divide and conquer mechanism, which means that we are going to split the original problem into multiple sub problems. We are going to solve these sub problems one by one in a parallel manner. And finally, we are going to combine the sub-solutions. By the way, this is exactly what we have been talking about in the previous chapter when we have been talking about parallel merge sort, the parallel sum operation and so on. We divided the problems into smaller and smaller sub-problems. We have managed to solve these sub-problems in a parallel manner simultaneously. And then we combined these sub-results. So this is a divide and conquer mechanism. Larger tasks can be divided into smaller ones. And of course, it is crucial that the subtasks have to be independent in order to be executed in parallel. Of course, this is exactly what we have been talking about when dealing with merge sort. The first part of the merge sort operations are independent, of course, after the first operation when we want to merge the sub results. Of course, it is a sequential approach, so this is why we had to wait for all of the threads to finish with the execution. So it is absolutely crucial that we are able to use parallelization for tasks that are independent of each other. So the main concept is that the Fork-Join framework breaks the tasks into smaller subtasks until these subtasks are simple enough to solve without further break ups. Usually, this is when we use the sequential approach. If you recall merge sort, we have used the exact same approach. Whenever the problem is large, we use parallelization and when the problem is small. For example, with parallel merge sort or with parallel maximum finding and so on. OK, so let's talk about the framework related classes and interfaces. 7050180.title: Sequential merge sort introduction
7050180.transcript: In this chapter and in the coming lectures, we are going to talk about parallel algorithms. But of course, to be able to understand the real power of parallel algorithms. So first of all, let's talk about the theoretical background of merge sort. So of course, it is a sorting algorithm, which means that the aim of the algorithm is to sort an underlying data structure in ascending or descending order. This more sort of algorithm is a divide and conquer approach that was invented by John von Neumann back in 1945. It is a comparison based algorithm, which means that the algorithm relies heavily on comparing the items. Of course, there are non-comparison based approaches that can outperform comparison based ones. Anyways, this lecture is not on sorting algorithms, it is just to consider another divide and conquer approach. So merge sort has anO(NlogN) linearithmic running time complexity. It is a stable sorting algorithm, which means that it maintains the relative orders of items with equal values and it is not in place. This is one of the disadvantages of merge sort that it is not in place. It requires O(N) additional memory. So we will see during the implementation that we have to create a data structure with as many items as the items we want to sort. So this is why it has a linear memory complexity. Of course, there are several competitors of merge sort, such as heap sort and quick sort. So heap sort has the same running time complexity as merge sort, but heap sort doesn't need additional memory. OK, as far as quick sort is concerned, it is considered to be the fastest sorting algorithm. So an efficient quick sort implementation generally outperforms merge sort, by the way, merge sort is often the best choice for sorting linked list data structures, which means that in these cases, merge sort doesn't need any additional memory. OK, so let's take a look at the algorithm itself. Of course, it is a divide and conquer approach with recursive function calls. So first, we have to divide the array. We would like to sort into two subarrays recursively. And of course, we have these sort subarrays recursively as well with merge sort So we are going to divided the arrays into two subarrays until there is only a single item left in the subarrays. This is the so-called base case for this recursive implementation. And of course, a subarray with a single item is considered to be sorted by default or by the way another good approach is to use insertion sort when the size of the subarray is smaller than 10, for example, because insertion sort of works extremely fine with small subarray containing less than 10 or less than five items. 9663786.title: What is the Stream API?
9663786.transcript: In this chapter and in the coming lectures, we are going to talk about the Stream API. So first of all, streams were introduced starting in Java 8. Essentially, streams introduce functional programming to the Java programming ecosystem. By the way, in computer science, functional programming is a programming paradigm where programs are constructed by applying and composing functions. Okay, then we know that streams rely heavily on lambda expressions. Lambda expressions are similar to methods, but they do not need a name and they can be implemented right in the body of a given method. By the way, we are going to talk about these concepts in the coming lectures. And finally, it is extremely easy to implement a given algorithm in parallel with the help of streams. So this is why stream API is extremely convenient to use. So essentially a stream is a sequence of items from a given source that supports data processing operations. It is extremely crucial that data structures are about storing items. So for example, if we are dealing with a one dimensional array or a binary search tree or a hash map, essentially these data structures are about how to store the given items. On the other hand, streams are about computation on the given data set. So data structures are about storing items, streams are about computations. So this is how we can do database like operations and common operations from functional programming languages such as filter, map and reduce. We are going to talk about all of these methods in the coming lectures. By the way, stream operations can be executed either sequentially or in parallel, and we can come to the conclusion that we don't have to bother with multiple threads. We don't have to deal with thread starvation, we don't have to deal with the Fork-Join framework to deal with these parallel threads. These are going to be handled by the Stream API. So it is going to be extremely convenient. One of the main features of streams is that we can define so called intermediate operations that returns with the stream as well, which means that it allows multiple operations to be chained. This is called pipelining and these are very similar to database queries. Okay. So first of all, we will have the source of a given stream, such as a given collection, for example, an ArrayList. So this is the source of the stream. Then there may be several intermediate operations such as filtering, sorting and so on. That's going to return with the stream as well. And finally there is the terminal operation that's going to return with a scalar value or a void such as the collect method, the reduce method and so on. We are going to talk about these methods one by one. Okay, So this is the big picture behind streams. And another important feature has something to do with internal iteration. So collections are iterated explicitly with iterator. This is called external iteration, but we can use a different approach which streams that is called internal iteration. We are going to talk about these features in the coming lectures. That's all about the theory behind streams. 14232158.title: What is time-slicing algorithm?
14232158.transcript: In the previous lecture, we have been talking about the fundamental basics of multithreading. We have been talking about processes and threads, and we came to the conclusion that there are multiple threads within a given application. So how these threads are going to be executed? And the answer has something to do with the time-slicing algorithm. So somehow the single central processing unit has to deal with all the threads in the application, and one approach is to use the time slicing algorithm. What's extremely crucial that here we assumed that there is just a single processor. Of course, in modern computers there's a huge number of available CPUs. For example, on my machine I can run up to 18 threads at the same time. But in this case we assume that there is just a single processing unit, and this is called the time slicing algorithm that essentially the processing time for a single processor is shared among multiple processes or threads. Then it will handle the second thread, then the first thread again, then the second thread, the first thread, the second thread and so on. Okay. So this is how multiple threads are handled with the help of a single processor. Usually modern computers have multiple processors or multiple processor cores, so when you run a multi threaded application, you may come to the conclusion that you end up with something like this. Essentially, both of the threads are executed at the same time. So when there are multiple processors or multiple processor cores, then all the threads can be executed in a parallel manner because the operating system is going to assign a distinct thread to every single central processing unit. And because there are multiple available processors in your machine, this is why all the threads are going to be executed at the same time. Essentially this is called parallelization. This is what we are going to talk about in the coming lectures. But what's crucial is that several students pose the question that despite the fact that they use multithreading, these threads are executed in parallel. And the answer is that you have multiple processor cores. So in this case, these threads are going to be executed at the same time simultaneously. If there are multiple threads, then the number of processors, then of course the time slicing algorithm is going to be used, which means that a given processor is going to handle multiple threads. 22509138.title: Why to use thread pools?
22509138.transcript: In this lecture we are going to talk about thread pools. But first of all, we have to talk about what is the problem when we try to create a large amount of threads manually. So when we manually create and manage a lot of threads using the new keyword. So when we instantiate a thread manually, especially in large scale and high throughput applications, we can run into several problems. First problem has something to do with high memory consumption. So each Java thread requires memory for its stack, typically 512kB to 1MB per thread, depending on the Java virtual machine and the platform. So this is the first problem that we have to handle. Then of course, we have to handle high CPU overhead because each thread has its own execution context. When many threads compete for CPU, the operating system must context switch between them. That is quite a time consuming operation. Then we have to deal with manual life cycle management. So we must handle starting new threads, joining these threads if necessary, which means that sometimes we have to wait for a given thread to finish execution. We have to handle exceptions and so on. And by the way, there are other disadvantages as well. For example, there is a risk of resource leaks. So if we forget to join or handle exceptions properly, that can lead to threads that never terminate. Of course it leads to memory and resource leaks. Then when we are dealing with threads manually, it is quite hard to recover from failures. So if one thread throws an exception and dies, there is no automatic recovery, there is no retry mechanism, there is no rejection policy so the system can become unstable or partially functional. Then it is quite difficult to optimize because manual threads give you no control over the maximum concurrency level. The thread reuse, task prioritization, and so on. On the other hand, when we are dealing with thread pools, it can give you these options out of the box. So this is why usually, and this is the case in the Java programming language, we should avoid manual thread management. And we should use thread pools instead. So this is exactly why we need thread pools. So a thread pool is a collection of worker threads that are reused to execute many tasks. Instead of creating a new thread for every task which is quite expensive, tasks are submitted to a pool and threads pick up and execute them. So Java provides thread pools through the executor service Interface, and its implementations and threads are created once and reused for multiple tasks. It reduces overhead of frequent thread creation and destruction. And by the way, if you are familiar with databases, it is very similar to connection pooling because a connection to a database is extremely expensive. So what modern frameworks such as the Spring Boot Framework does is that it creates several connections, and whenever there is a database operation that, given connection that is already created, is used for a specific query. This is very similar with thread pools, where threads are created once and reused for multiple tasks, and if all threads are busy, submitted tasks are queued. They are held in a blocking queue data structure until a thread becomes available. So the big picture is that we have the thread pool with multiple worker threads. And of course we have the tasks. So there may be several tasks. And of course the threads that are present in the thread pool keep executing the tasks one by one. By the way, waiting tasks are stored in a blocking queue, typically in a linked blocking queue or a synchronized queue. This queue holds tasks waiting to be executed when no thread is immediately available. You may pose the question that how the threads are stored exactly in the thread pool. And the answer is that the thread pool maintains an internal pool of worker threads in a collection, like a hash set or other internal structure. These threads wait for tasks to arrive from the task queue and run the task when one is available. So these are the data structures under the hood that the Threadpool uses, and there are the tasks that are being executed by these worker threads one by one. By the way, there are several types of thread pools. First of all, we can create a new fixed thread pool where n is the number of threads that we want to create. In this case, the thread pool is initialized lazily, so threads are not created at the start, but they are created on demand up to the core pool size. In this case the input parameter. So n is the core pool size that defines the number of threads. And of course, as you may guess that once all threads are created and busy, new tasks are put into the queue and wait until a thread becomes available. So when you submit a task, it is either executed immediately if a thread is free or it is stored in a queue. If all threads are busy and of course threads pull from the queue when they finish their actual task. And of course the threads are reused, not destroyed after completing tasks. Then what about cached thread pool? In this case, the thread pool creates new threads as needed and reuses previously constructed threads when available. This approach automatically removes idle threads after a short timeout. The default value is 60s. What does it mean that if a thread is idle for more than 60s, it is removed from the thread pool, which means that there's going to be fewer threads? Because we assume that there are fewer tasks we have to handle. And of course, it is designed for applications that launch many short lived asynchronous tasks. And when those tasks are finished. Then, of course, there is no need for the large number of threads. So this is why this approach removes idle threads after a short timeout. Then we have the so-called single thread executor. This creates a thread pool that uses only one worker thread to execute tasks sequentially, and it uses an unbounded queue in order to store the tasks. And by the way, this is a huge disadvantage because since it uses a linked blocking queue with no limit, if tasks are submitted faster than they are executed, such as with long running tasks, the queue can grow indefinitely. There is a potential memory pressure and the application may run out of memory. So this is why we have to be very careful when dealing with single thread executors. And if this thread dies because of an exception. A new thread is created automatically. And finally we have the scheduled thread pool. It creates a thread pool that can either schedule tasks to run after a delay, or schedule tasks to run periodically. There is a risk of thread starvation, which means that if your core pool size that is defined by this n variable is small and schedule tasks are long running, then new schedule tasks may be delayed. And this is exactly what starvation means that a thread or a given task has to wait indefinitely to be executed. Use it when you need tasks to run on a timer or at regular intervals. By the way, in the coming lectures, we are going to talk about all of these types one by one. And finally, I would like to tell a few words about the advantages, although there are a huge number of Advantages of the executor service and Java thread pool. First of all, it supports future and callable, which means that you can submit tasks that return values using the callable interface, and the result is wrapped in a future object. Then automatic thread lifecycle management is available, so you don't need to manually manage start and join, which means that we don't have to start a thread explicitly. We don't have to call the join method and wait for a given thread to finish with the execution. The executor handles everything such as the creation, the execution, and the shutdown of threads. Then there is thread reuse. So threads are created once and reuse for multiple tasks. It reduces overhead of frequent thread creation and destruction. By the way, there are several other extremely powerful features, such as Graceful shutdown. We are going to talk about that. How to shut down all the threads within a thread pool, and we will come to the conclusion that it is extremely powerful. We can prevent resource leaks and also clean up termination in an extremely convenient manner. And finally it introduces scalability. So using a thread pool allows your application to scale to handle many tasks without creating thousands of threads. Why it matters because we can end up with a more responsive and efficient application, especially under load. And of course, applications in the 21st century must be extremely responsive, highly available and scalable. So this is why multi-threading matters. And this is why we have to talk about thread pools and executor services in the coming lectures.

29730826.title: Amdahl's law
29730826.transcript: In the previous lectures, we have been talking about parallelization and how to design parallel algorithms. And in this lecture we have to talk about Amdahl's law that defines the limitation of parallelization. So first of all, this flow describes the maximum speed up you can expect when only part of a program can be parallelized. And you may pose the question that why do we have to bother with something like that? Because in real world applications, not all the operations can be parallelized. For example, if we consider merge sort, it is a divide and conquer algorithm. The divide part can be run in parallel, but the merge operations are sequential by default. It is sequential because it involves comparing elements from two sorted subarrays and combining them into a new array. This is inherently order dependent, so you must look at both arrays, actual elements to decide which goes next. And Amdahl's law states that the speedup of a program using multiple processors in parallel computing is limited by the sequential portion of the program. So even if you use 100 processors, if 10% of your program is sequential, that can be parallelized. That part becomes a bottleneck. So let's take a look at the mathematical formulation. It defines the speedup. And this is a function of the number of processors or processor cores which is equals to one divided by one minus p plus p divided by n. And of course n is the number of processors or cores. As we discussed earlier, capital P is the fraction of the application that can be parallelized, and one minus P is the fraction of the application that cannot be parallelized. So this is the sequential part that must run on one processor. And we can come to the conclusion based on this formula that as you increase the number of processors, if your sequential part is too large, you will hit a hard limit. For example, if 10% of your code is sequential, the maximum theoretical speedup is ten x, no matter how many processors you use. So we can take a look at the chart. It is present on Wikipedia. And we can come to the conclusion that, for example, if 50% of the application can be parallelized and 50% is sequential by default, we cannot do better than to x, so we can double the speed of the application, but we cannot do any better. And we can come to the conclusion that if 95% of the application can be parallel and only 5% of the application is sequential by default, which means that cannot be parallelized, then we cannot exceed 20 x no matter how many processors we use. As you can see, even if we use 65,000 processors, we cannot exceed 20 x as the speed up. So this slow is crucial because in theory, we may come to the conclusion that okay, if we have a problem, we just have to use parallel computing because modern computers have multiple processors or processor cores. But it is a bit more complicated because usually an application has a part that must be executed sequentially, and it defines an upper limit for the speed up. That's all about Amdahl's law. Thanks for watching.

34323774.title: Collections and streams
34323774.transcript: In this lecture, we are going to talk about the fundamental differences between collections and streams. So let's take a look at collections. Collections are essentially data structures in the memory. For example, an array list, a long list, a binary heap, a red black tree, a binary search tree. These are standard data structures in order to store data in the memory. What's extremely crucial that collections need the entire data in advance. Every element in the collection must be computed before it can be ordered to the collection. So this is a fundamental feature of collections that we have the entire data set from which we are going to build the underlying data structure. Of course, this is exactly why it takes more time to handle a collection than a stream, because we need all the data, all the items that we are going to be stored in the given collection. On the other hand, streams provide interfaces to data structures representing a sequenced set of values like, for example, video frames being streamed over the Internet. Streams are fixed data structures whose elements are computed on demand. This is why streams are essentially lazy collections. So it is absolutely crucial that the items of a given stream are going to be computed on demand in a lazy manner, which means that no matter that how many intermediate operations we are going to execute, such as filtering, sorting, mapping and so on, these operations are not going to be executed immediately. These are going to be executed right before the terminal operation. And by the way, we are going to talk about it, that this is exactly how Java is going to optimize the streams, and this is why streams are way faster than standard collections. Okay. So the items are computed on demand in a lazy manner. This is why streams are way faster. So for example, if we want to fetch all the prime numbers, then if we are using collections, for example a standard array list, then calculating all these prime numbers takes infinite time because we have to consider all of the prime numbers one by one. So essentially we are going to end up with an infinite for loop. On the other hand, if we are using streams, then finding all the prime numbers can be done easily because the items are calculated when needed. This is what we have been talking about that the items in a given stream are going to be computed on demand. So we are not going to compute these values immediately, but we are going to compute them on demand in a lazy manner. So this is why it is possible to find all the prime numbers with streams. And by the way, a given collection can be traversed as many times as we want, while on the other hand a stream can be traversed only once. So, for example, if we have an array list of strings Adam and and Kevin, these are the names, then we can call the Stream method. And the stream method is going to return with a stream of strings. This is how we transform a collection into a stream, essentially. And then we can use the for each method that's going to consider every single item and prints these items one by one. 41069258.title: Continuations and coroutines
41069258.transcript: In this lecture we are going to talk about continuation and coroutines. And finally we will understand how virtual threads are implemented under the hood. So first of all, let's talk about standard methods or subroutines. Whenever we call a subroutine, the entry point is the beginning of that given subroutine or method, and everything that is included will be executed on a line by line basis. And finally, when the subroutine ends, it will return to the caller method. Sometimes subroutines can return with a given value. In Java, we cannot return any value, so essentially we just return to the caller method. This is a typical subroutine. What about coroutines? A coroutine is very similar to a subroutine, but instead of just a single entry point, the beginning. Essentially, there may be several entry points. So first, when we call the run method on the coroutine, then the entry point is the beginning of that coroutine. But if we call the yield method within the coroutine, essentially we will return to the caller method. And whenever we call the run method again it is not going to start over, but we continue where we left off before calling the yield method. And essentially we continue executing the coroutine whenever there's another yield method. Then the coroutine returns to the caller method again. So essentially there may be multiple entry points for a given coroutine. So we can come to the conclusion that coroutines may have multiple entry points. Whenever we call the run method, then we can return to the previous state where we left off in the coroutine, we can freeze the running of the actual process with the yield method. This is when we return to the caller. Of course, we have to store the stack frames with local variables and previous coroutine calls on the heap memory. This is how we can return to the given entry point within the coroutine, and in Java it is called continuation. So continuation is the object that keeps the stack and code pointer, so the stack can be recreated when the run is called again. And of course you may pose the question that why are we talking about subroutines, coroutines and continuations? And of course, because it has something to do with virtual threads. Essentially, with the help of continuations, we can implement virtual threads, because this is exactly what we have been talking about in the previous lectures, that when the virtual thread is blocked, then the Java virtual machine saves the state on the heap memory and the platform thread or the career thread executes another virtual thread that is available. Okay, so now another virtual thread is executed, and in some point in the future, the Java virtual machine again will continue executing the given tasks that are in that given virtual thread. And this is exactly what happens. We don't want to re-execute everything again, but we want to get back to where we left off. And this is exactly what can be implemented with the help of continuations. Whenever the virtual thread starts running, then essentially we keep executing the lines of the coroutine line by line. Whenever there is a blocking point, then the continuation calls yield. It means that the coroutine returns, and whenever the block is over, then we can call run again and we continue the execution of that given coroutine. So with the help of continuation, we can achieve virtual threads, and we can make sure that the underlying operating thread is not going to be blocked. Thanks for watching.

42652148.title: What are virtual threads?
42652148.transcript: In this lecture and in the coming lectures, we are going to understand virtual threads. But if we want to understand virtual threads, first we have to talk about monolithic architecture and microservice architecture. So first of all, what is the monolithic application or the monolith architecture. It means that essentially we are dealing with a single application with all the features, all the services, all the repositories and database related code and so on. And essentially we build the whole application into a single .jar file or .war file that will be deployed onto a server. The application is a single unit. You may guess that this is not the best approach possible, because if we have a minor problem with a given feature, then essentially we have to re-deploy the whole application again. And during the deployment the users cannot use the application. This is why microservice architecture came to be, which means that we split the monolith application into independent units. We have a shipping service. As you can see, all the shipping service related features: services, database related code were included in the monolith application. And essentially we take those features, we take the services, the database related code etc. and we create an independent application. Essentially, it is called a (micro)service that can be deployed independently. Then we have the inventory service that can be deployed independently, the login service that can be deployed independently. And this is exactly why it is so powerful. Because if there is a small issue in the shipping service, then we have to update the shipping service exclusively. We have to run the tests. We have to deploy just that single service, and there is no need to deploy all the other services. Essentially, this is why it is a good approach to break the single unit into independent chunks. This is exactly the microservice architecture. So we can come to the conclusion that modern applications are independent microservices that are communicating with each other with the help of HTTP. This is exactly what's happening with all the modern applications. Facebook, Instagram and all the applications are essentially microservices under the hood. So we can come to the conclusion that most modern applications have to communicate a lot with other services and with the database under the hood. And this is exactly the problem, because these operations, these are called input output operations, such as communicating with other microservices with the help of HTTP or making database related queries. These input output operations are usually quite slow, and the actual application has to wait a lot for these input output operations. So the conclusion is that if we use threads, then these threads will have to wait for other operations to finish. And for most of the times these threads are idle. And of course, this is not an efficient way of utilizing threads and the underlying central processing units. So let's take a look at a concrete example. So for example we have an HTTP endpoint to get all the vehicles. This is why it is a GET mapping. I'm not sure whether you are familiar with microservice architecture. I have an independent course on this topic. But anyways, whenever we call a given URL a given endpoint, then this method will handle that given get mapping. And what we are going to do is that first of all, we communicate with an independent microservice, the vehicle microservice, and we fetch the user associated with that given vehicle. As you can see, it is an I/O operation because we have to wait for the service to make the HTTP call and fetch the user, then we are going to validate the user. For example, this is a database query and again it is an input output operation, which means that we have to wait for the database to finish with that given query. And finally we get the vehicle from the database which is another input output operation. And we return with the result. So what does it mean exactly? If we use an independent thread, a standard Java so-called platform thread in order to do these operations, then for most of the time the thread will have to wait for input-output (I/O) operation because it waits for the HTTP response in order to fetch the user. Then it has to wait for database related operations, and finally it has to wait for another database related query. So if we use a standard platform thread, a standard Java thread that we have been dealing with in the past lectures, then essentially for most of the time this thread will have to wait. And of course it is not good because under the hood, the operating system related thread will have to wait for these operations to be finished because of the input output operations that block the given thread. What if we are using virtual threads? If we are using a single virtual thread, the virtual thread will be blocked as well. Because of these input output operations of course. But what's extremely crucial that virtual threads are handled and managed by the Java virtual machine, and every single virtual thread is executed by a standard Java thread, a platform thread, or a so-called carrier thread. And as you can see, a thread is not associated with I/O operations. So essentially the JVM will use platform threads in order to execute operations that are not blocking I/O operations. And whenever the virtual thread has to wait under the hood, the platform thread is released and it doesn't have to wait for the input output operations to be finished, which means that it can execute other operations and after the blocking input output operation, the JVM will find an available platform thread to finish the given operation. So this is the logic behind virtual threads that whenever there is blocking I/O operations under the hood, when the underlying virtual thread has to wait, then the JVM can release the underlying platform thread, which means that given thread will not be idle, it is not going to wait for no reason. Of course, you may pose the question that what does it mean exactly? What are virtual threads? What are carrier threads? How it is going to be executed under the hood? This is exactly what we are going to discuss in the next lecture. Thanks for watching.

We can make sure that, for example, we do this operation if there is no problem. We cannot notify the parent, for example, that there is some problem because when we use unstructured concurrency, for example, asynchronous approach, then we start a given thread and then we keep going. 51674297.title: Is multithreading faster than sequential execution?
51674297.transcript: In this lecture, we are going to talk about an interesting topic that is a common interview question whether sequential execution is faster or when using multiple threads. So let's take a look at the problem itself. Let's assume that we are dealing with sequential execution. So the central processing unit executes the first operation for example the first method. And after that the second method in a sequential manner. What if we are using multiple threads. In this case, just for demonstration purposes, we are using two threads. In this case, the time slicing algorithm is going to execute these operations on a step by step basis. So the central processing unit executes a portion of the first task. Then it switches to the second task, then it gets back to the first task and so on. By the way, the central processing unit processes a given slice for approximately ten milliseconds, and after that it switches to the other thread. So the first conclusion that in theory it is exactly the same. So no matter we are dealing with sequential execution or multi-threading of course, because the operations are splitted into smaller parts. This is why the final running time will be approximately the same. But of course, in real world multi-threading might be slower due to context switching. So context switching is when a central processing unit stops executing one task or one thread and switches to the other. So of course, as you may guess, whenever the central processing unit stops executing the first thread related operation and it switches to the second thread related operation. Of course, it takes some time. And of course there are several context switching like that. So this is why multi-threading may be slower, because the central processing unit must save the state of the actual thread, so the registers, the program counter and so on. It has to load the state of the next thread to be executed. And finally it updates internal bookkeeping such as memory mapping, scheduling, queues, and so on. So context switching is quite an expensive operation. This is exactly why it is not always a good solution to introduce multiple threads into the application. By the way, as you may guess, and this is exactly why we are going to talk about parallel computing. Because in the 21st century, there are multiple CPUs or CPU cores in a given computer. And of course, if there are multiple CPUs, then the operating system can assign every thread to independent CPUs, which means that whenever we use multi-threading, essentially the operations are executed in parallel. That will be faster, of course, but if we assume that there is just a single central processing unit, then introducing multiple threads into the application is not going to make the application faster, but it makes the application slower due to context switching. And of course, you may pose the question that if multi-threading is usually slower, then why use multi-threading? First of all, because of concurrency. Multi-threading allows a program to do multiple things at once. For example, a general user interface application can remain responsive while loading data in the background, or a server can handle multiple client requests simultaneously. Then it improves resource utilization, so multi-threading helps utilize idle CPU time, especially in input output bound applications. So, for example, while one thread waits for a slow disk or network operation, another thread can keep the CPU busy. So the final conclusion is that it is not a good idea to use multiple threads. We have to use multiple threads if needed, and if we want the application to do multiple things at once.

51819471.title: Locks and ReentrantLocks introduction

It is called re-entrant because the thread that holds the lock can acquire it again without getting blocked. You may come to the conclusion that there's going to be some problem, because the thread that acquired the intrinsic lock because of the synchronized keyword for the first method, is essentially waiting for itself to release that given lock to be able to acquire it again. As you may guess, if Java's synchronized were not re-entrant, the above examples would cause a deadlock because the thread would block waiting for a lock it already holds. So this is why in Java there are re-entrant locks, so the thread can acquire the same lock multiple times without any problem. And this re-entrant look implementation has the same behavior. So these are re-entrant locks by default. There are several crucial methods of this re-entrant lock class such as Trylock method. It tries to acquire the lock without blocking. On the other hand, if a thread tries to enter a synchronized block or synchronized method and the lock is held by another thread, it will wait indefinitely until the lock becomes available. So this is a fundamental difference between the two approaches that with synchronized blocks the thread will wait no matter what it is going to wait until the lock becomes available. With reentrant locks, we can try to acquire the lock without blocking. This is the trilogue method. Then we have the lock Interruptible method that allows a thread to be interrupted while waiting for the lock. It is not possible with the synchronized approach. And then there is the new condition method that creates a condition object for more advanced thread coordination. It is similar to wait and notify, but it is way more powerful. Then I would like to tell a few words about fairness. So whenever we instantiate a new re-entrant look, we can define a boolean value. True or false? If it is true, it means that the look is fair. So the first thread will be served first if the boolean value in the constructor is false. This is when we end up with unfair looks. This is the default value by the way, and it means that there is no guaranteed order. This is why there is a non-deterministic behavior when dealing with synchronized blocks. And by the way, usually we use the boolean value. True Because we would like to allow fairness. By the way, what are the advantages and disadvantages? If the Boolean value is true, which means that we are dealing with fair re-entrant locks, then threads acquire the lock in the order they requested it, which means that we eliminated the non-deterministic behavior of synchronized blocks, because now the order in which the threads acquire the lock is well defined. Of course, this is how we can prevent starvation of threads. And of course, there is slightly slower performance due to the overhead of maintaining a queue, because of course we have to store all the threads that are waiting for that intrinsic lock to be released. So there is an additional data structure under the hood that we have to handle. This is why if we set the boolean value to be true, then we end up with slower performance. So we have to use fair locks when we want predictable behavior and we want to avoid thread starvation. By the way, if you may post the question that what is thread starvation? It means that a given thread is waiting for the intrinsic lock to be released indefinitely, and the Java virtual machine always handles the lock to another thread. And this is why the given thread that is waiting for that lock indefinitely will starve. If we use fair locks, we can avoid this thread starvation. What if we set this boolean value to be false so we end up with unfair locks? In this case, threads can jump the queue if the lock is available when they request it. So again we end up with a non-deterministic behavior. We are not sure what thread will acquire the intrinsic clock. It is random. It may result in higher throughput due to fewer context switches, but some threads may starve.
